perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means: (i) technologies in written or tangible form disclosed to COMPANY from time to time after the Effective Date of this Agreement by ROCHESTER and identified with particularity at time of disclosure and marked co nfidential; and (ii) technolo gies communicated orally or visually to COMPANY by ROCH ESTER, if it is reduced to writing or other tangible form by ROCHESTER on or before the date thirty days after the date of such communication, marked confidential, and promptly delivered to COMPANY.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means: (i) technologies in written or tangible form disclosed to COMPANY from time to time after the Effective Date of this Agreement by ROCHESTER and identified at time of disclosure; and (ii) technologies communicated orally or visually to COMPANY by ROCHESTER, if it is reduced to writing or other tangible form by ROCHESTER on or before the date ninety days after the date of such communication, and delivered to COMPANY.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the requirements for technologies to be 'identified with particularity' and 'marked confidential', as well as extending the period to reduce orally disclosed information to a tangible form from 30 to 90 days. This creates a broader, less defined scope of what constitutes 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' broadens its scope, potentially including information that doesn't meet the Economic Espionage Act's requirement of being 'secret' and having commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through proper means. The law requires specific measures to protect the confidentiality of the information; removing the 'marked confidential' requirement weakens this protection and could make it harder to prove the information was intended to be a trade secret under the law.
perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement for it to be 'identified with particularity' and 'marked confidential'. The law explanation states that this weakens the protection of the information and could make it harder to prove it was intended to be a trade secret under the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, indicating its relevance. Removing the 'marked confidential' requirement directly impacts the measures taken to protect the confidentiality of the information, which is a critical aspect of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: COMPANY agree that, for a period of five (5) ye ars after the date of its receipt of the last Confidential Information disclosed under the Agreement, it shall: (i) keep Confidential Information confidential; and (ii) not use the Confidential Information for any commercial purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: COMPANY agree that, for a period of one (1) year after the date of its receipt of the last Confidential Information disclosed under the Agreement, it shall: (i) endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential; and (ii) not directly profit from the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one, replaces the strict 'keep Confidential Information confidential' with a weaker 'endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential', and changes 'not use for any commercial purpose' to 'not directly profit from the Confidential Information'. This significantly weakens the protection of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secret Law (N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Trade Secret Law protects against misappropriation of trade secrets. By significantly reducing the confidentiality period and weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, this modification reduces the legal protections for Rochester's confidential information, making it more vulnerable to misappropriation. The switch to 'endeavor' introduces ambiguity, making it harder to prove a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 350 | FindLaw New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 350. perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, contradicting the implied protection against misappropriation under New York Trade Secret Law as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text ensured confidentiality for five years and prohibited any commercial use. The changed text reduces the period to one year, weakens the confidentiality obligation to 'endeavor to keep confidential,' and changes the restriction to 'not directly profit,' creating ambiguity and reducing protection. The modified contract is weaker than the original regarding the confidential information and may not hold up under legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing shall not apply to that part of any Confidential Information that: (c) at any time becomes generally known to the public through no fault of COMPANY; or (d) has been or is made available to COMPANY by a third party having the lawful right to do so without breaching any obligation of nonuse or confidentiality to ROCHESTER; or (e) has been or is disclosed to others by ROCHESTER without similar restrictions on disclosure and use; or (f) COMPANY is required to disclose pursuant to an order of a judicial or administrative authority
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing shall not apply to that part of any Confidential Information that: (c) at any time becomes known; or (d) has been or is made available to COMPANY by a third party; or (e) has been or is disclosed to others by ROCHESTER; or (f) COMPANY believes it is required to disclose pursuant to an order of a judicial or administrative authority.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes key qualifiers from the exceptions to confidentiality. Specifically, it removes "through no fault of COMPANY" from (c), "having the lawful right to do so without breaching any obligation of nonuse or confidentiality to ROCHESTER" from (d), "without similar restrictions on disclosure and use" from (e), and replaces "is required to disclose" with "believes it is required to disclose" from (f). This significantly broadens the exceptions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - adopted by New York as N.Y. CPLR § 4548
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By removing the qualifiers from the exceptions to confidentiality, the modified text allows COMPANY to disclose confidential information even if the disclosure is due to their own negligence or a third party's breach of confidentiality. This contradicts the UTSA's intent to protect trade secrets and could make it more difficult for Rochester to prove misappropriation.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly broadens the exceptions to confidentiality, weakening the protection of confidential information. By removing qualifiers like "through no fault of COMPANY" and "having the lawful right to do so without breaching any obligation of nonuse or confidentiality to ROCHESTER," the new text permits disclosure under circumstances that would previously have been prohibited. The change from "is required to disclose" to "believes it is required to disclose" further weakens the protection, making it subjective. While the snippets provided aren't directly relevant to establishing a contradiction based on specific language in the UTSA, the Perturbation and Law Explanations make it clear that the removal of qualifiers undermines the intent of protecting trade secrets, suggesting a strong legal contradiction, as it makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: ROCHESTER authorizes COMPANY to disclose the Confidential Information to those of its employees and consultants who require the Confidential Information for the evaluation hereunder, and to potential licensees, prov ided each such employee, consultant an d potential licensee has first entered into a written agreement in which it agrees to be bound by similar obligations of nonuse and nondisclosure as those imposed on COMPANY hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: ROCHESTER authorizes COMPANY to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees and consultants who require the Confidential Information for the evaluation hereunder, and to potential licensees, provided each such employee, consultant and potential licensee verbally agrees to maintain the confidentiality of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a written agreement for employees, consultants, and potential licensees. The modified text replaces this with a verbal agreement. This significantly weakens the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Privacy Protection Law (N.Y. Public Officers Law § 91)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this law primarily focuses on government agencies and their data handling, the principle of requiring documented consent for the handling of sensitive information is a key theme. Moving from a written agreement to a verbal agreement weakens the demonstrability of proper consent and adherence to confidentiality, contradicting the broader legal trend towards verifiable data protection, and further impacting the enforceability of privacy agreements.

perturbation.justification: The change from requiring a 'written agreement' to a 'verbal agreement' for confidentiality directly undermines the enforceability and demonstrability of that agreement. The Perturbation Explanation states this weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation supports that a 'written agreement' shows verifiable data protection. While the scraped snippets are not directly applicable, they point to the importance of personal privacy protection laws, which is undermined by a verbal agreement. This weakens the original intent to protect confidentiality because verbal agreements are difficult to prove and enforce in court compared to written contracts, therefore a legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and enforced under the internal laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and enforced under the principles of equity and fairness.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by New York law. The modified text removes this, replacing it with the vague standard of "principles of equity and fairness."
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law allows parties to specify New York law as governing in contracts over $250,000. By removing the reference to New York law, the contract becomes ambiguous about which jurisdiction's laws apply. This could make the contract more difficult to enforce in New York if it is below that amount and the parties have connections elsewhere, potentially undermining the predictability and enforceability intended by this section of the law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code , may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code . 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that New York law governs the agreement, while the changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness.' According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to agree that New York law shall govern their rights and duties in contracts over $250,000. By removing the reference to New York law, the contract becomes ambiguous about which jurisdiction's laws apply. This creates a legal contradiction because it deviates from the legally recognized method of specifying governing law, making the contract potentially unenforceable under New York law, especially if it meets the criteria outlined in GOB § 5-1401.
